title: Wives, Husbands, and Suffering for Righteousness
subtitle: Submission in marriage, blessing enemies, and the hope that defends

heb.paragraph: Wives, be subject to your own husbands - the same verb used for submission to government (2:13) and slaves to masters (2:18). The motivation here is evangelistic.
Respectful and pure conduct - the behavior of wives may win disobedient husbands without a word. Conduct speaks louder than speech.
The weaker vessel - likely referring to physical vulnerability, not moral or intellectual inferiority. Husbands are to show honor.

refs.note: Wives, submit to your own husbands, as to the Lord.
Wives, submit to your husbands. Husbands, love your wives.
The unbelieving husband is made holy because of his wife.
Charm is deceitful and beauty is vain, but a woman who fears the LORD is to be praised.

notes.note: Wives, submit to husbands so that even unbelieving husbands may be won without a word by the respectful and pure conduct of their wives.
Do not let your adorning be external - braiding of hair, gold jewelry, clothing. Let your adorning be the hidden person of the heart.
Holy women who hoped in God adorned themselves by submitting to husbands, as Sarah obeyed Abraham, calling him lord.
Husbands, live with your wives in an understanding way, showing honor to the woman as the weaker vessel, as heirs together of the grace of life.

Calvin notes that the goal is evangelism. A believing wife may convert an unbelieving husband not by nagging but by holy conduct. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

Husbands must honor wives. The weaker vessel language does not imply inferiority but vulnerability requiring protection. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase without a word suggests that conduct is more persuasive than argument when dealing with hostile unbelievers.
Sarah calling Abraham lord (Genesis 18:12) is cited as an example of respectful submission.
Prayers being hindered suggests that marital strife disrupts spiritual life. Harmony at home affects communion with God.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes that the submission ethic is not patriarchal oppression but a strategy for witness in a patriarchal culture. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes notes that in Roman society, wives were expected to follow their husbands' religion. Christian wives who did not faced social pressure. Jobes attends to the socio-rhetorical context of the passage, noting how the author addresses a community under social pressure. The language of identity, belonging, and hope is calibrated to sustain faith amid marginalization — the letter functions as pastoral care for believers navigating a hostile cultural environment.

hist.historical: Peter writes to Christians in five Roman provinces of Asia Minor (modern Turkey) — Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia. These believers face social ostracism and verbal abuse (4:4, 14) rather than official state persecution. As 'elect exiles' (1:1), they experience the marginalization of those who no longer participate in pagan civic religion, trade guild feasts, and family worship. The letter's household codes (2:13–3:7) address how Christians should live within Roman social structures. Peter writes from 'Babylon' (5:13) — almost certainly a symbolic name for Rome. Silvanus (Silas) serves as secretary (5:12), and Mark is present (5:13). The letter likely dates to the early 60s AD, before Nero's persecution.
hist.context: Peter continues the household code begun in chapter 2. Wives are to submit to husbands - even unbelieving ones - so that they may be won by conduct rather than words. The emphasis is on inner beauty rather than external adornment. Husbands are to live with wives in an understanding way, showing honor as fellow heirs of grace, so that prayers may not be hindered.

heb.paragraph: Be like-minded - unity of thought and purpose in the community. Not uniformity but harmony.
Sympathetic - literally suffering together. Sharing in one another's experiences, both joys and sorrows.
Do not repay evil for evil or reviling for reviling, but on the contrary, bless. The Christian response to hostility is benediction.

refs.note: Bless those who persecute you; bless and do not curse.
The eyes of the LORD are on the righteous, and his ears are open to their cry.
Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you.
A soft answer turns away wrath.

notes.note: Be like-minded, sympathetic, loving, tender, humble. Do not repay evil for evil or reviling for reviling, but bless.
Peter quotes Psalm 34: keep your tongue from evil, turn away from evil, do good, seek peace. The eyes of the Lord are on the righteous.

Calvin sees blessing enemies as the hardest command. Natural instinct is retaliation; grace produces benediction. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase that you may obtain a blessing connects conduct with inheritance. Those who bless others will themselves be blessed. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

The face of the Lord is against evildoers - not indifference but active opposition. God takes sides. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes that the virtues are corporate. The community must function as a harmonious body, not a collection of individuals. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes emphasizes that Psalm 34 is a wisdom psalm. Peter grounds ethics in Israel's wisdom tradition. Jobes attends to the socio-rhetorical context of the passage, noting how the author addresses a community under social pressure. The language of identity, belonging, and hope is calibrated to sustain faith amid marginalization — the letter functions as pastoral care for believers navigating a hostile cultural environment.

hist.context: Peter summarizes the ethical section with five virtues: unity, sympathy, brotherly love, tender heart, humble mind. The community is to be characterized by harmony, not retaliation. Psalm 34:12-16 is quoted: whoever desires to love life and see good days must keep the tongue from evil and do good. The eyes of the Lord are on the righteous.

heb.paragraph: Always be prepared to make a defense (apologia) to anyone who asks for the reason for the hope that is in you. Apologetics is mandated.
Keep your conscience clear - a good conscience enables bold witness. Accusations will be shamed when conduct is blameless.
Christ proclaimed to the spirits in prison - a notoriously difficult passage. Christ announced victory to imprisoned spiritual beings.

refs.note: Blessed are those who are persecuted for righteousness sake.
Do not fear what they fear; do not be in dread. But the LORD of hosts, him you shall honor as holy.
The sons of God saw the daughters of man - background for spirits in prison.
Baptism into Christ's death and resurrection.

notes.note: Who can harm you if you are zealous for good? Even if you suffer for righteousness, you are blessed. Honor Christ; be ready to make a defense.
Keep a good conscience so that accusers are put to shame. It is better to suffer for doing good than for doing evil.
Christ suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous. Made alive in the spirit, he proclaimed to spirits in prison who disobeyed in Noah's days.
Baptism now saves you - not removal of dirt but an appeal to God from a good conscience. Christ has gone into heaven, with angels subject to him.
Calvin emphasizes that suffering for righteousness is blessed, not cursed. The world's opposition is a mark of divine favor.
Calvin interprets the spirits in prison as souls from Noah's generation to whom Christ preached through Noah. Others see angelic beings.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The term apologia is a legal term for defense in court. Christians must be able to articulate the rationale for their hope.

The spirits in prison is one of the most debated passages in the NT. Options include: (1) Christ preaching through Noah; (2) Christ descending to Hades; (3) Christ proclaiming victory to fallen angels.
Baptism saves as a pledge (eperotema) - a commitment or appeal to God. The efficacy is through Christ's resurrection.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner argues that the spirits are fallen angels imprisoned in the time of Noah. Christ proclaimed his victory over them after his resurrection. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes connects Noah's ark to baptism: both involve passing safely through waters of judgment. The few saved through water prefigure baptismal salvation. Jobes attends to the socio-rhetorical context of the passage, noting how the author addresses a community under social pressure. The language of identity, belonging, and hope is calibrated to sustain faith amid marginalization — the letter functions as pastoral care for believers navigating a hostile cultural environment.

hist.context: Peter addresses suffering for righteousness. Who can harm those who are zealous for good? Even if they suffer, they are blessed. They must not fear but honor Christ and be ready to defend their hope with gentleness and respect. Christ suffered for sins once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring us to God. He was put to death in flesh but made alive in spirit, in which he proclaimed to spirits in prison - those who disobeyed in Noah's day. Baptism now saves as an appeal to God from a good conscience, through the resurrection of Christ who has gone into heaven.

themes.note: Justice and Mission are strong: suffering for righteousness, defending hope. Sovereignty appears in Christ's cosmic victory.

lit.note: Chapter 3 completes the household code (wives/husbands) and addresses suffering for righteousness, culminating in Christ's victory.

discourse.thesis: Wives and husbands, live honorably — always be prepared to give a defense for the hope within you.

nodes.text: Wives, be subject to your husbands so that even if some do not obey the word, they may be won without a word by their conduct. Let your adorning be the imperishable beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit. Sarah obeyed Abraham — you are her children.
Husbands, live with your wives in an understanding way, showing honor as heirs together of the grace of life, so that your prayers may not be hindered.

Finally, all of you: have unity of mind, sympathy, brotherly love, a tender heart, a humble mind. Do not repay evil for evil or reviling for reviling — bless instead. The eyes of the Lord are on the righteous.

Who will harm you if you are zealous for good? But even if you suffer for righteousness, you are blessed. Do not fear them. Always be prepared to give a defense for the hope in you — with gentleness and respect.

Christ suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring you to God. He was put to death in the flesh but made alive in the spirit — he went and proclaimed to the spirits in prison. Baptism now saves you — through the resurrection of Jesus Christ, who is at God's right hand.

coaching.tip: 'Wives, be subject to your own husbands, so that even if some do not obey the word, they may be won without a word' (v. 1). The context is mixed marriages — believing wives with unbelieving husbands. Peter's strategy: conduct preaches when speech can't. Holy living evangelizes resistant spouses. Behavior can be more persuasive than argument.

'Always being prepared to make a defense to anyone who asks you for a reason for the hope that is in you' (v. 15). This assumes outsiders are asking. Suffering believers attract curiosity: Why are they hopeful amid hardship? Apologetics isn't aggressive argument but responsive explanation. Questions create opportunities that lectures don't.
